sentences.text: My name is Moses Goldberg, also known as Maurie Goldberg.
I spell-- Moses Goldberg's spelled M-O-S-E-S.
My family name spells G-O-L-D-B-E-R-G.
I was born December 25, 1926.
I was born in a city Stary Sacz.
Spells S-T-A-R-Y-S-A-C-Z.
And the country was Poland, P-O-L-A-N-D.
I celebrated my 69th birthday about a week ago.
My father's name was Yehoshua Selig Goldberg.
He was a merchant.
He was earning a store of shoes-- shoe-- leather shoes-- and shoemaker's [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] merchandise.
My father comes from the city of Czarnow in Poland.
He came-- he came from a very Hasidic family.
And, of course, after he got married, he kept up all the same religion.
And he was a Hasid of Shinever dynasty, which-- which originated from the sons of [NON-ENGLISH] in Nowy Sacz.
He was active in the synagogue.
He was [NON-ENGLISH] for a time.
He was also one of the officers who are running the-- all kinds of activities in the shul.
[NON-ENGLISH] means that you read the Torah during the week and on Saturday, which you have to pronounce it with certain vowels.
And you have to really be educated to read the Torah.
As far as I remember, I-- since I-- I remember as a young child, I was going to the synagogue twice a day, once in the morning, and once in the evening.
My mother's name was Cyla, C-Y-L-A.
And her maiden name was Englander.
My mother was born in Nowy Sacz with very known-- very-- family.
Englander family was very well known in the city.
Was one of the prestigious families in Nowy Sacz.
She was a mother.
She had eight children.
And she was occupied mostly in the house, and she was also helping my father in the business.
She did a lot of charity work.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We have-- once-- once in every second week, there was a market in the city.
And a lot of Jewish people came to sell their goods.
When those people came, a lot of people who were poor, who were came to collect money.
She used to cook a big pot of soup.
She served them every-- when lunch time came.
When she died, they found out, they said, where are we going to eat?
Who's going to feed us now?
She did a lot of other charity work.
She was a wonderful person.
I lost her when I was 12 years old.
Right.
Well, I had a very happy life.
I was the youngest of my brothers and sisters.
Two of my sisters, I remember, were already married.
And two sisters and three brothers were home.
They spoiled me very, very much, because I was the youngest at home.
I had a very happy life as a child, I still remember.
I was going to public school in the morning, from 8 o'clock till 1:00.
Then I came home, and I had lunch.
Then I went to cheder.
Cheder is a Jewish school where we learned mostly Torah and Chumash, the Bible, and all the others things.
And then, in the evening when I came home, I used to do the homework.
Well, we had no televisions at that time, so we did the best we can.
But as far as I remember, I had a very happy childhood.
My sisters and my brothers were always there to help me.
My oldest sister's name was Sarah.
I remember her already as a married woman.
She didn't live in our town.
She lived in another town.
She lived in Bielsko.
This was near the German border.
My other sister's name was Lotti.
She also was married.
I remember her only married.
I don't remember her at home anymore.
My third sister was Chana.
She was my favorite sister.
She used to help me with my homework.
And whenever I had a problem, I went to her.
My mother used to be most of the times sick.
So all the things, when I had problems, I went to her.
My third sister-- my fourth sister, excuse me, was name was Mala.
She was the youngest of the sisters.
And they were all devoted to each other, and also to me and my father.
And she used to be helping my mother in the cooking.
So everybody has a job to do.
She also used to help in the store.
And I remember when I used to-- we were young, I used to go out and play with the boys and play soccer.
And a lot of things that young boys do.
And you had brothers?
And then I had three brothers.
My oldest brother's name was Samuel.
He was one of-- he was much older.
And he was working in the-- in the-- in the wood where they-- see, we have a lot of forest around our town.
So when the people cut the forest, and they brought the wood-- so they cut it in all kinds of shapes.
He was working in this kind of division.
My younger-- my second brother's name was Baruch.
He was one of the Hasidic boys in our family.
He was a Bobover Hasid, which is means it's a Bobover dynasty.
Which the-- we have now the Bobover Rebbe Schlita, lives here also in Brooklyn.
He later became-- he went to Bielsko, where my sisters lived.
And he became working in a printing office-- printing factory, whatever it is.
They used to make all kinds of letters, headings.
And my third brother was-- was name was Benek.
He was like three years older than me.
So we use to fight a lot.
And he also wound up in Bielsko, working for another company there.
And what was left in our house was my mother, my father, my sister, the youngest Mala, and me.
The rest of the family were away.
Well, before the war, there were about 500 Jewish families.
I mean, 500 people-- Jewish people, excuse me.
There were about 150 families, right.
And see, there was a small town.
So it was like a family.
Everybody knew everything was going on in somebody else's house.
My neighbor knew what my mother was cooking for lunch.
Everybody lived together.
And when we came together in shul, it was-- I still remember, that it's-- it was-- you know, everybody was together.
And it was a very nice life.
Because it was a small town.
And we live-- we lived together in the neighborhood from other people.
And one had the store with the grocery, one had the fish store.
So we had to support everybody.
My-- my mother used to shop for Shabbos-- she used to buy flour in this store, and she used to buy sugar in the other store.
And she bought other things, challahs, somewhere else.
Everybody should be able to make a living.
We were supporting each other, because it was a small town and everybody depended on the other guy.
There were very few rich people in our hometown.
I could count on the fingers, maybe five or six family families which were really considered rich people.
The rest of the people were middle class, and a lot of them were very poor.
There were families that we have to support them with money.
Which I remember as a boy, I used to go out with a list every Thursday to each store, and everybody gave $0.25, or [NON-ENGLISH], whatever it is-- a zloty, to support the families.
And we divided it later in-- in-- In a certain amount of money.
Well, in-- in Poland, the-- the relationship wasn't so good, especially in the small towns.
They're very big anti-Semites.
I remember going to cheder.
After school, I had to go around another block because there was a [NON-ENGLISH] there who was throwing stones at us.
And there were certain-- there a lot of-- the population-- from the goyish population, was very anti-Semitic.
Especially when there was a Jewish-- goyish holiday, like Christmas or Easter, a Jew could not go out to the street.
It was afraid to go out.
With the stores, we had to be closed.
And I went to school, there was a public school, We went together with the Christian boys.
And when we came the recess, especially in the winter when they were throwing snow-- snowballs at us, and doing all kind of mischief.
You see, here in America, the young boys fight back-- we had-- didn't have that mentality to fight back, to the-- to do something back to them.
That's why they did-- we were defenseless.
They were the majority, and we were just a few boys.
I remember we had to be in the class when-- when they had to sing the-- the songs for Christmas.
I knew all the songs by heart.
Because we had-- they forced us to sit in the class and listen to their-- to their songs.
So we had to-- not only, we had to sing along with them, too.
And that teacher who taught those songs was big anti-Semite, too.
And he just made us in spite.
But we survived it, so it wasn't so bad.
Did they used to-- did the Polish boys call the Jews names?
Oh, yes.
They called us [NON-ENGLISH].
It means Jews to go to Palestine.
And all kind of names.
The [NON-ENGLISH]-- it means dirty Jew, whatever it is.
They did a lot of problems, a lot of things to us.
And as I said before, we were afraid to go some places at night, because of those boys.
They-- they hated Jews.
They said in-- in Poland-- in Poland, that a woman who was nursing a child, a Christian woman, she was nursing the hatred from-- from her milk, to hate the Jews.
They-- they-- see, they had their priests in-- in the churches were-- were preaching against the Jews.
That's the hatred that came.
See, all the concentration camps and all the ovens and what they killed the Jews, were all in Poland.
They brought the Jews from Budapest, from Czechoslovakia, from Romania, only to Poland.
There was no Auschwitz in Hungary or Treblinka.
Everything was in Poland, because they knew the Polish people will help them to do it.
And they did.
Well I said, they used to beat us up.
I didn't see anything.
They beat up the older people, they wouldn't touch.
Because they were afraid for their parents.
But they used to make us a lot of trouble.
Gentiles?
I would say about 3,000.
We were a minority there.
No.
They lived all around the town.
We lived in the-- in the [NON-ENGLISH], which calls the plaza, which was the main business section.
But the Jews lived all over.
Some people who could not afford, lived in-- in-- the outskirts of the-- of the town.
But they lived among the Polish.
We lived all scattered around.
Later, when-- when the war broke out, so they put us all to-- in one or two, three streets.
Well, this was one of the nicest holidays for us, because my brothers used to come home.
So we were all together.
But the preparation for the holiday was something that you don't-- you cannot describe unless you see yourself.
My mother used to start already like seven weeks before, to prepare for Passover.
We used to take out all our Jewish books, the [NON-ENGLISH] and the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, all the Hebrew books-- we put them outside on a-- in the-- in the yard on a piece of wood.
And the wind used to open up the-- the pages, so we should not find a piece of-- piece of bread in it.
So everything came out.
This was-- we used to put them out in the morning and take them back at night.
And what was going on in the house, you know-- you couldn't eat here, you couldn't eat there.
But all the cleaning.
It wasn't like here-- you have all the dishes for Passover.
We had Passover dishes, but there's certain, like, silverware you had to kosher.
So we had to cook or boil very hot water with-- with charcoal coals in it.
That's how you kosher the silverware.
It was-- it was something that you felt that the holiday came.
Because mostly what you did, you made at home.
There was no such a thing to go into grocery and buy anything.
Everything that you-- that you ate for Passover, you made at home.
I remember, we used to bake our own matzohs.
We used clean up the room from the-- from the-- the bedroom, and the living room, from the furniture and put big tables on it.
Because we were a big family.
And the neighbors came and helped us, and they-- they baked their matzohs, too.
We had a special oven for matzohs, which only used it once a year.
And we had a whole box of matzohs, so was plenty to eat.
But there was a lot of work.
My mother, she used to get sick night after Passover, because from all that work that she did.
But it was fun.
And everybody loved it, especially the holidays.
We ate all kind of food.
You know, everything made home.
It was very nice.
Well my brothers used to come home, because there were three brothers.
And my two sisters were home.
Sometimes my married sister came with the children.
It was-- you know, you looked forward to it.
You stay-- you made the Seder one of the nicest room.
And my father was-- was like a king laying on a couch.
And he was-- brought everything to his-- to his table.
And we were-- I was the youngest. and I asked for the [NON-ENGLISH], the four questions.
And it was-- it was a holiday that you looked forward to.
Then you had special food, which you don't eat all the year round.
Especially, you know, in Poland.
So we really, really liked it very much.
It was very nice.
We looked forward to the Jewish holidays.
And shul, you know, when we went to the synagogue, I still have the picture of every Friday night when I go to-- to the synagogue.
I see all the people who used to pray.
And I still remember where they used to sit, what their names, and everything.
Which, it's hard.
Well, it's in 1939.
Actually, the Germans came in September.
But we already felt before what's going to be.
So what happened-- my mother-- my mother was not alive anymore.
My mother died in 1938.
She suffered from gallstones.
And at that time, they didn't operate like-- like they operate now.
So she got sick, very sick on Rosh Hashanah.
And between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, we took her to the hospital, to Krakow, which we had no hospital in our town.
I remember my sister took her, and she took a friend and one of my friends-- she shouldn't go alone.
And my-- the last word-- I went with my mother to the-- to the station, to the railway station.
The last words she said, take care.
And then, about six days later, we got to know that she's gone.
She wanted to come-- the last day, she wanted to be-- she said to my sister, I want to go home.
I want to die at home.
My sister took her out from the hospital-- she was still alive.
And in Poland, you couldn't take a dead person in a train, because you had to have a special permission.
But they put her in a-- in a wagon where they had-- where they put all the packages and everything.
And she died going home.
And my sister make her to believe that she's still alive, because she used to give her to drink, and she was already dead.
And I went with my father to shul in the morning to say [NON-ENGLISH].
It was two days before Yom Kippur.
And one of the friends told my family, you better close the store and go to-- to Nowy Sacz-- which is a bigger town.
Your wife is dead.
She's dying.
That was a big tragedy.
My-- my mother was 55 years old.
But I said, look, after that, she at least died in the regular course.
She was buried with a Jewish tradition.
It was lucky for her.
And when it came, to when the Germans started to come into-- to Poland, they can gradually from the German border.
And to Stary Sacz they came in September, 1939.
Most of the people ran away.
They were going to the east, towards Russia.
We left the town, my father, my sister, and myself, almost on the last minute.
When we crossed the bridge, and we went by foot to the bigger town to Nowy Sacz.
We crossed the bridge, and they burned the-- the Polacks burned the bridge, the Polish army, because they want to make it harder for the Germans to come into the city.
As we were approaching the biggest city, Nowy Sacz, there was a man who has a store-- a liquor store.
He says, why are you going to Nowy Sacz?
There's nobody left.
Everybody left Nowy Sacz.
There's nobody there.
So he said, there's a train just going to the east.
Why don't you go there?
So we went along.
And we went-- we traveled about two days.
And we came to the city of Czechow.
And my father-- we had an aunt in Czechow, her name was also Goldberg.
And we got off the train and we went to her house.
As soon as we came, she says she's leaving the house.
She's going to a village, to a Christian family.
She doesn't want to be here.
She's afraid for the Germans.
We stayed in that house til the Germans came.
And there were a lot of bombs falling down.
And every time there was an air raid, we had to run to the basement.
But the Germans came in there and they made a lot of trouble.
I remember one day, I was in the house.
The Germans came in looking for people to work.
I was like 13 years old.
And they have-- my sister and my father was afraid they should take me away.
So they put me in bed.
They put a big kerchief on my mouth that I have a toothache.
But they took my father to work.
He worked for a day and he came back.
He was very exhausted.
We stayed in Czechow for about four weeks.
It was hard to get food right away, and everything.
But things started to normalize a little bit.
So after four weeks, we rented a wagon-- a horse wagon.
And we went back to Stary Sacz.
When we came to our town, our apartment was already cleaned up.
A Christian family wanted to take it-- take over.
And they put all our furniture in one room.
My sister knew some people in the-- in the government, in the Polish, in the-- in the city.
She went in there and she paid some money and they made-- we got back our apartment.
We stayed in Stary Sacz in our original apartment, I would say, til 1941.
Beginning of 1941, we had to evacuate the apartment because it was in the main-- in the main street.
We got an apartment somewhere.
We had three, four rooms.
We got an apartment, a little kitchen-- in a-- in a Christian family.
And all our furniture was staying outside.
So we just were able to put two beds.
And-- and this was-- a little window.
This was like a little-- a little box.
That's how we had to live for a while, because this was in the section where they were putting all the Jews.
They took them out from all the biggest streets and they're putting them in there.
OK.
It was-- was very bad.
Because, you see, they took the Jews to, every-- every Tuesday, you had to go to a certain place.
And you had to report there.
And they designated what the Jews should do.
They used to clean the streets, wash the latrines in the-- in the-- by the Germans, and do all kind of degrading work.
My father was already like 60 years old.
He was past 60.
And-- and they didn't take me yet, because I was young.
So I remember, I used to go and stay outside of the plaza where the Jews were there.
And if they took my father to work, I went instead of him.
He went home, and I went to-- to the work for him.
And every time I was able to do it.
Till they started to take me to work, already, too.
So then it was harder.
But you see, we couldn't attend the synagogue anymore.
We had to make special [NON-ENGLISH].
